title: Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy
concept: Pixel-wise cross-entropy evaluates loss for each pixel independently, but suffers from class imbalance in typical segmentation masks.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How do we optimize DeepLab for mobile devices?

options.text: By swapping a heavy backbone (like ResNet) with a lightweight one (like MobileNet)
By adding a Conditional Random Field (CRF) post-processor

exercises.explain: Correct! Lightweight backbones are essential for mobile devices.

cards.title: The background bias
cards.body: Imagine an image where 99% of pixels are black background and 1% is a tiny tumor. If a model predicts 'background' for every single pixel, it gets 99% accuracy! A standard classification loss function would consider this a perfect score, even though the model completely missed the tumor. We need to look at pixel loss differently.

cards.title: Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy
cards.body: Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy Loss evaluates the cross-entropy loss for each pixel independently and averages them over the image. It treats every pixel as an independent classification task, regardless of class size.

cards.title: Grading a spelling test
cards.body: Imagine a spelling test with 99 easy words and 1 hard word. If you grade by just counting correct words, a student who skips the hard word still gets a 99%. Pixel-wise cross-entropy suffers from this same bias toward the easy background pixels.

cards.title: Class imbalance failure
cards.body: In road segmentation, the road might cover 10% of the pixels, and buildings cover 90%. A model trained with standard cross-entropy might struggle to detect the road boundary because the buildings dominate the loss score.

cards.title: Weighted CE is a partial fix
cards.body: You might think we can't use cross-entropy at all. We can, by using **Weighted Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy**, which multiplies the loss of rare classes (like the road or tumor) by a scaling factor to force the model to pay attention.

cards.title: Standard vs. Weighted Cross-Entropy
cards.body: How class weights shift model focus:

left.text:
Rare class pixels have low impact on loss
Model predicts background easily
Poor segmentation on small details

right.text:
Rare pixels are multiplied by weight (e.g. 10x)
High penalty for missing small objects
Better class balance

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of standard pixel-wise cross-entropy loss?

options.text: It suffers from bias when there is extreme class imbalance
It cannot be computed on GPUs
It is only compatible with binary segmentation

exercises.explain: Correct! When one class dominates, standard cross-entropy ignores rare classes.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Weighted cross-entropy gives more importance to common background pixels.
exercises.explain: False. It scales up the loss of rare classes to balance their impact.

exercises.sentence: Pixel-wise cross-entropy treats each ___ as an independent classification task.
exercises.explain: It calculates loss pixel-by-pixel, ignoring neighbor context directly in the math.

exercises.prompt: Why does a model trained on a highly imbalanced dataset with standard cross-entropy output empty masks?

options.text: Because predicting all background pixels yields a very low loss value
Because the learning rate is set to zero
Because the model has too many skip connections

exercises.explain: Since background pixels dominate, the model learns the lazy trick of predicting background everywhere.

exercises.prompt: You are segmenting thin crack lines on concrete walls. The cracks cover 0.5% of the image. What will happen if you train using standard pixel-wise cross-entropy?

options.text: The model will likely predict no cracks at all, as it can achieve 99.5% accuracy by classifying every pixel as wall background.
The model will predict cracks everywhere, coloring the entire wall.
The training speed will drop to zero frames per second.
The model will automatically convert the image to 3D volumetric data.

exercises.explain: Perfect! This is the classic class imbalance trap. Standard loss fails because the background wall pixels overwhelm the small crack pixels. Next: let's look at an overlap-based loss function!
